sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=60, col=30).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 127, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 126 actions; a strong score needs about 36 or fewer. You are 3.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
All cells are gray again! The click at (60, 30) didn't work. But the previous clicks at (55, 25), (50, 30), and (55, 35) did work.
Let me check the cursor position. Maybe the cursor moved to a position where clicks don't work.
Looking at the bar, the cursor (S) is at cols 35-36, which is further left than before. The cursor seems to be moving left with each action.
Wait, let me check the cursor position more carefully. The bar shows:
- Left marker: SSSS at cols 13-16
- Cursor: S at col 35 (or maybe cols 35-36)
The cursor has moved from cols 39-40 to cols 35-36. This is a shift of 4 columns to the left.
Maybe the cursor position determines which cells are clickable. When the cursor is at a certain position, clicking affects different cells.
Let me check the cursor position after each successful click:
1. Click at (55, 25) → cursor at cols 39-40 → (1, 0) turned green
2. Click at (50, 30) → cursor at cols 37-38 → (0, 1) turned green
3. Click at (55, 35) → cursor at cols 35-36 → (1, 2) turned green
4. Click at (60, 30) → cursor at cols 33-34 → (2, 1) didn't turn green (all cells gray)
Wait, that doesn't match. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the bar in the current image, the cursor (S) is at cols 35-36. But after the click at (60, 30), the cursor should have moved further left.
Let me check the cursor position more carefully by reading the ASCII.
Actually, I think the issue is different. Let me re-examine the click behavior:
- When I click a cell, it turns green.
- But when I click the next cell, all cells turn gray.
This suggests that clicking a cell resets all other cells to gray. So I can only have one green cell at a time.
But earlier, I had multiple green cells: (0, 1), (1, 0), and (1, 2) were all green. Then I clicked (2, 1), and all cells turned gray.
Wait, that doesn't match either. Let me re-examine the history.
Looking at the output after clicking (55, 35):
- (0, 1): N (green)
- (1, 0): N (green)
- (1, 2): N (green)
So 3 cells were green. Then I clicked (60, 30), and all cells turned gray.
This suggests that clicking (2, 1) reset all cells to gray. Maybe (2, 1) is a special cell that resets the board.
Or maybe the cursor position affects the click behavior. When the cursor is at a certain position, clicking resets the board.
Let me check the cursor position after each click:
1. After click at (55, 25): cursor at cols 39-40
2. After click at (50, 30): cursor at cols 37-38
3. After click at (55, 35): cursor at cols 35-36
4. After click at (60, 30): cursor at cols 33-34
The cursor moves left by 2 columns after each click. When the cursor reaches a certain position, clicking resets the board.
Actually, I think the issue is that the cursor is moving left, and when it reaches the left end of the bar, clicking resets the board.
Let me try moving the cursor back to the right end and then clicking (2, 1).
But wait, I don't know how to move the cursor. The valid actions are UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE. Maybe I can use LEFT/RIGHT to move the cursor.
Actually, looking at the bar, the cursor is at cols 35-36. The left end is at cols 13-16. The right end is at cols 41-42.
Let me try using RIGHT to move the cursor to the right end, and then clicking (2, 1).
But first, let me check the cursor position by reading the ASCII.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# All cells gray again. Let me check the cursor position and try to understand the pattern.
curr_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
bar_line = curr_lines[19]
print("Bar line:", repr(bar_line[10:45]))

# Check cursor position
print("\nCursor positions:")
for j in range(12, 50):
    if bar_line[j] == 'S':
        print(f"  col {j}")

# Try moving cursor right and then clicking (2, 1)
print("\nTrying RIGHT to move cursor...")
action(['RIGHT'])

bar_line2 = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')[19]
print("Bar line after RIGHT:", repr(bar_line2[10:45]))
print("\nCursor positions after RIGHT:")
for j in range(12, 50):
    if bar_line2[j] == 'S':
        print(f"  col {j}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
